elements.sent-2411-001: Bowen Yu is affiliated with Qwen Team, Alibaba Group.
elements.sent-2411-002: Jie Lou is affiliated with Xiaohongshu Inc.
elements.sent-2411-003: This work is initiated by 4 corresponding authors in a personal meeting.
elements.sent-2411-004: The evolution of machine learning has increasingly prioritized the development of powerful models and more scalable supervision signals.
elements.sent-2411-005: However, the emergence of foundation models presents significant challenges in providing effective supervision signals necessary for further enhancing their capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-006: Consequently, there is an urgent need to explore novel supervision signals and technical approaches.
elements.sent-2411-007: In this paper, we propose verifier engineering, a novel post-training paradigm specifically designed for the era of foundation models.
elements.sent-2411-008: The core of verifier engineering involves leveraging a suite of automated verifiers to perform verification tasks and deliver meaningful feedback to foundation models.
elements.sent-2411-009: We systematically categorize the verifier engineering process into three essential stages: search, verify, and feedback, and provide a comprehensive review of state-of-the-art research developments within each stage.
elements.sent-2411-010: We believe that verifier engineering constitutes a fundamental pathway toward achieving Artificial General Intelligence.
elements.sent-2411-011: The evolution of machine learning~\cite{jordan2015machine has undergone a progressive journey characterized by the pursuit of increasingly powerful models and the scaling of supervision signals~\cite{friedland2018capacityscalinglawartificial,cao2024scalableautomatedalignmentllms,Sevilla_2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Over the past decades, both model capacity and the scale of supervision signals have escalated in a synergistic manner.
elements.sent-2411-013: Powerful models necessitate more scalable and effective supervision signals to fully utilize their parameters.
elements.sent-2411-014: Conversely, the expansion of supervision signals requires models with enhanced capacities to effectively exploit these signals, thereby achieving more generalized capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-015: In the early stages of machine learning, models were constrained by their limited capacity.
elements.sent-2411-016: This period was characterized as feature engineering, during which domain experts manually designed and extracted relevant features.
elements.sent-2411-017: Classical algorithms, such as Support Vector Machines~\cite{hearst1998support and Decision Trees~\cite{quinlan1986induction , relied heavily on meticulously crafted features due to their architectural limitations.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: These algorithms achieved optimal performance through carefully designed feature extraction techniques, with Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency~\cite{robertson2009probabilistic serving as a prominent example.}
elements.figure-2411-001: 0.48\textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{p{2.0cm}p{3.0cm}p{3.2cm}p{3.0cm}} Feature Engineering Data Engineering Verifier Engineering Representative Models Machine Learning\newline Models Deep Learning\newline Models Foundation Models e.g. SVM, XGBoost CNN, LSTM LLMs, VLMs Supervision Manual Features Human Annotations Verifier Feedback Scope Task-Specific Multiple Related Tasks General Intelligence Generalization Limited Relatively high High Scalability Limited Moderate High \end{tabular}} \caption{Comparison of feature engineering, data engineering, and verifier engineering} \label{tab:compare}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{figures/framework-v4.pdf} \caption{\textbf{Framework of verifier engineering}: The fundamental stages of verifier engineering include Search, Verify, and Feedback. Given an instruction, the process begins with generating candidate responses (Search), followed by evaluating these candidates using appropriate verifier combinations (Verify), and concludes with optimizing the model's output distribution (Feedback). This framework can explain various approaches, from training-based methods like RLHF~\cite{ouyang2022training} to inference-based techniques such as OmegaPRM~\cite{luo2024improve} and Experiential Co-Learning~\cite{qian2023experiential}. We systematically categorize existing approaches into these three stages in Table~\ref{tab:exist-work}.} \label{fig:framework}
elements.sent-2411-019: However, as addressed increasingly complex problems, the limitations of manual feature engineering became more pronounced, underscoring the need for more scalable approaches to construct features.
elements.sent-2411-020: The emergence of deep learning~\cite{SCHMIDHUBER201585 approximately two decades ago marked a transformative shift, inaugurating the data engineering era.}
elements.sent-2411-021: This new paradigm represented a fundamental departure from handcrafted features, emphasizing instead the curation of high-quality datasets and annotations to facilitate automated knowledge acquisition and pattern recognition across diverse domains and tasks.
elements.sent-2411-022: The remarkable success of landmark projects such as ImageNet~\cite{deng2009imagenet and BERT~\cite{brown2020language validated the effectiveness of this data-centric approach.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Unfortunately, the emergence of foundation models in recent years~\cite{ouyang2022training,touvron2023llama,BetkerImprovingIG,dosovitskiy2021imageworth16x16words has made it increasingly difficult to enhance model capabilities solely through data engineering.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Specifically, foundation models, particularly large language models (LLMs)~\cite{ouyang2022training,touvron2023llama , have demonstrated extraordinary abilities, increasingly matching or surpassing human performance across various domains.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Nevertheless, the traditional data engineering approach of augmenting these models through large-scale data construction has reached its practical limitations.
elements.sent-2411-026: This limitation is evident in two primary challenges: the difficulty and unsustainable costs associated with high-quality human annotations for post-training~\cite{anthropic2024measuring,burns2023weaktostrong , and the complexity involved in providing meaningful guidance that can further enhance model performance~\cite{wen2024language .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Consequently, the central challenge in the current era is determining how to supply more effective supervision signals to foundation models to achieve general artificial intelligence capabilities.

IPO \cite{azar2024general}; KTO~\cite{ethayarajh2024kto} etc., probing_work] ] [Reinforcement Learning, probing [PPO~\cite{schulman2017proximal}; PPO-max~\cite{zheng2023secrets}; RLMEC~\cite{chen2024improving} etc., probing_work] ] ] [Inference Based, [Verifier Guided , probing [PRM \cite{lightman2023let}; BON \cite{sun2024fastbestofndecodingspeculative}; RAIN \cite{li2024rain}; etc., probing_work] ] [Verifier Aware, probing [Self-Debug \cite{chen2023teaching}; ReAct \cite{yao2022react}; Experiential Co-Learning \cite{qian2023experiential}; Each stage is further broken down into specific approaches, with references to notable works in each area.} \label{fig:tax}
elements.sent-2411-028: In this paper, we propose verifier engineering, a novel post-training paradigm designed for the foundation model era.
elements.sent-2411-029: The essence of verifier engineering lies in extending the construction of supervision signals beyond traditional manual feature extraction and data annotation.
elements.sent-2411-030: Instead, it utilizes a suite of effective automated verifiers to perform verification tasks and provide meaningful feedback to foundation models.
elements.sent-2411-031: Table~\ref{tab:compare delineates the key distinctions among feature engineering, data engineering, and verifier engineering.}
elements.sent-2411-032: This progression from annotate and learn to search and verify signifies a fundamental advancement in enhancing the capabilities of foundation models.
elements.sent-2411-033: Compared to preceding paradigms, verifier engineering streamlines the creation of verifiers and facilitates efficient feedback to foundation models through automated verification processes.
elements.sent-2411-034: Specifically, given an instruction, verifier engineering initiates by generating candidate responses, subsequently verifying these candidates using appropriate combinations of verifiers, and ultimately optimizes the model's output distribution.
elements.sent-2411-035: Unlike existing methodologies such as Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)~\cite{stiennon2020learning,ouyang2022training , which depend on a limited set of verifier sources and feedback mechanisms, verifier engineering integrates multiple diverse verifiers to deliver more accurate and generalizable feedback signals.}
elements.sent-2411-036: By shifting the improvement of foundation models from a data-centric endeavor to a systematic engineering challenge, verifier engineering emphasizes the design and orchestration of complex verification systems to ensure effective and efficient feedback.
elements.sent-2411-037: Analogous to how feature engineering and data engineering achieved scalability in their respective eras, we posit that verifier engineering represents a crucial step toward the advancement of general artificial intelligence.
elements.sent-2411-038: To this end, this paper provides a comprehensive exploration of the verifier engineering landscape, breaking it down into three core stages: search, verify, and feedback.
elements.sent-2411-039: These stages are defined as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-040: Search: Sampling representative or potentially problematic samples from model output distribution to reveal performance boundaries and limitations.
elements.sent-2411-041: Verify: Utilizing various verifiers to provide verification results on candidate responses, which may include evaluation metrics, rule detection, or selective manual annotation.
elements.sent-2411-042: Feedback: Leveraging verification results to enhance model performance through methods such as Supervised Fine-Tuning or In-Context Learning.
elements.sent-2411-043: To showcase the latest state-of-the-art research developments in these three stages, we provide a comprehensive review in the remainder of this paper: Section 2 presents the formal definition and preliminaries of verifier engineering.
elements.sent-2411-044: Sections 3-5 detail the three fundamental stages: search, verify, and feedback.
elements.sent-2411-045: Section 6 discusses current trends and limitations, followed by concluding remarks in Section 7.
elements.sent-2411-046: In this section, we formalize verifier engineering as a Goal-Conditioned Markov Decision Process (GC-MDP)~\cite{pmlr-v37-schaul15, plappert2018multigoalreinforcementlearningchallenging, liu2022goalconditionedreinforcementlearningproblems , enabling a unified and systematic perspective on the field of verifier engineering.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Then, we introduce how the concepts of search, verify, and feedback correspond within this modeling framework, and analyze them through examples.
elements.sent-2411-048: Furthermore, we provide a comprehensive overview of the verifier engineering landscape by categorizing existing post-training approaches into three core stages, as summarized in Table~\ref{tab:exist-work .}
elements.sent-2411-049: While LLMs are typically trained to maximize generation likelihood given input, this objective alone cannot guarantee desired post-training capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-050: To bridge this gap, we formalize verifier engineering as a GC-MDP, denoted as tuple (\(S\), \(A\), \(T\), \(G\), \(R_g\), \(p_g\)), where: State Space \( S \) represents the model's state during interaction, including input context, internal states, and intermediate outputs.
elements.sent-2411-051: Action Space \( A \) represents the possible token selections at each generation step: \begin{equation}A = \{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_N\}\end{equation} Transition Function \(T\) typically defines the probability distribution over the next states, given the current state $s \in S$ and action $a \in A$.
elements.sent-2411-052: Specifically, in large language models, the state transition is a deterministic function.
elements.sent-2411-053: That is, once the current state and selected action (the generated token) are given, the next state is fully determined.
elements.sent-2411-054: The search stage of the verifier engineering thus can be regarded as exploration in the action-state space in the condition of \(T\).
elements.sent-2411-055: Goal Space \( G \) represents the various goals related to model capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-056: Each goal \( g \in G \) corresponds to a specific model capability, such as code, math, writing, and so on.
elements.sent-2411-057: The goal space is multi-dimensional and can encompass various aspects of model capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-058: Goal Distribution $p_g$ is the probability distribution over goals from the goal space $G$ .
elements.sent-2411-059: It represents the likelihood of a specific goal $g \in G$ being selected at any given time.
elements.sent-2411-060: This distribution can be learned from human feedback or other external signals.
elements.sent-2411-061: Reward Function \( R_g(s, a) \) or \(R_g(s')\) represents the reward the model receives given the goal \( g \), when taking action \( a \) from state \( s \) or at the transformed state \(s'\).
elements.sent-2411-062: This reward thus reflects the verification result of verifier engineering for the specific goal \( g \).
elements.sent-2411-063: For example, if the goal \( g \) is "fairness," the reward might be based on whether the generated text avoids bias.
elements.sent-2411-064: The objective for improving model capabilities can be defined as a goal-conditioned policy $\pi : S \times G \times A \rightarrow [0, 1]$ that maximizes the expectation of the cumulative return over the goal distribution. \begin{equation}J(\pi)=\mathbb{E}_{\substack{a_t \sim \pi\left(\cdot \mid s_t, g\right), g \sim p_g s_{t+1} \sim \mathcal{T}\left(\cdot \mid s_t, a_t\right)}}\left[\sum_t \gamma^t R_g\left(s_t, a_t\right)\right]\end{equation} The reward function can be decomposed into sub-functions for different capability dimensions: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} R_g(s, a) = \mathcal{F} \left(R_{g,i}\left(s, a\right) \mid i \in \mathcal{S}_g \right) \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{S _g$ represents selected sub-functions for goal \(g\), and $\mathcal{F}$ combines their evaluations.}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}J(\pi)=E_{\substack{a_t \sim \pi\left(\cdot \mid s_t, g\right), g \sim p_g s_{t+1} \sim T\left(\cdot \mid s_t, a_t\right)}}\left[\sum_t \gamma^t R_g\left(s_t, a_t\right)\right]\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation} R_g(s, a) = F \left(R_{g,i}\left(s, a\right) \mid i \in S_g \right) \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-065: Following Probably Approximately Correct Theory (PAC)~\cite{Vapnik2000 , even with imperfect sub-functions, we can achieve reliable overall evaluation by combining multiple weak verifiers.}
elements.sent-2411-066: Building upon the GC-MDP framework, we demonstrate how the three stages of verifier engineering—Search, Verify, and Feedback—naturally align with specific components of this formalism.
elements.sent-2411-067: This mapping provides both theoretical grounding and insights into how each stage contributes to optimizing the policy distribution $\pi$ toward desired goals.
elements.sent-2411-068: As illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:framework , the search stage can be divided into linear search and tree search methods.}
elements.sent-2411-069: The verify stage selects appropriate verifiers and invokes them to provide verification results based on the candidate responses.
elements.sent-2411-070: The feedback stage improve the model's output using either training-based methods or inference-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-071: For example, RLHF utilizes linear search to generate batches of responses, employs a reward model as the verifier, and applies the Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) \cite{schulman2017proximal algorithm to optimize the model based on the reward model's verification result.}
elements.sent-2411-072: OmegaPRM \cite{luo2024improvemathematicalreasoninglanguage employs a process reward model as a verifier and searches for the best result based on the PRM by maximizing the process reward scores.}
elements.sent-2411-073: Experimental Co-Learning \cite{qian2023experiential collaborative verifiers through multiple LLMs, enhancing both verify and model performance through historical interaction data.}
elements.sent-2411-074: In the GC-MDP framework, the three stages manifest as follows: Search corresponds to action selection, where state $s$ represents the current context or partial sequence, and action $a$ denotes the next token$s$ to generate.
elements.sent-2411-075: Verify maps to the reward function $R_g(s, a)$, evaluating how well the generated outputs align with the specified goal $g$.
elements.sent-2411-076: Feedback relates to policy distribution $\pi$ optimization, maximizing the expected cumulative return $J(\pi)$ based on verification results.
elements.sent-2411-077: Search aims to identify high-quality generated sequences that align with the intended goals, forming the foundation of verifier engineering, which is critical for evaluating and refining foundation models.
elements.sent-2411-078: However, it is impractical to exhaustively search the entire state-action space due to its exponential growth—driven by the large vocabulary size \(N\) and the maximum generation length \(T\).
elements.sent-2411-079: To address this challenge, efficient searching, which seeks to navigate this vast space by prioritizing diverse and goal-oriented exploration, plays a critical role in improving model's performance.
elements.sent-2411-080: In this section, we first introduce how to implement diverse search from the perspective of search structure and discuss additional methods for further enhancing search diversity after the search structure is determined.
elements.sent-2411-081: Search structure denotes the framework or strategy used to navigate the state-action space, which significantly influences the effectiveness and efficiency of the search process.
elements.sent-2411-082: Currently, there are two widely adopted structures for implementing search: linear search and tree search.
elements.sent-2411-083: Linear search progresses sequentially, making it effective for tasks involving step-by-step actions, while tree search examines multiple paths at each decision point, making it well-suited for tasks requiring complex reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-084: Linear Search is a widely used search method where the model starts from an initial state and proceeds step by step, selecting one token at a time until reaching the terminal state~\cite{brown2020languagemodelsfewshotlearners,wang2024chain .}
elements.sent-2411-085: The key advantage of linear search is its low computational cost, which makes it efficient in scenarios where actions can be selected sequentially to progress toward a goal.
elements.sent-2411-086: However, a notable limitation is that if a sub-optimal action is chosen early in the process, it may be challenging to rectify the subsequent sequence.
elements.sent-2411-087: Thus, during the progress of verifier engineering, careful verification at each step is crucial to ensure the overall effectiveness of the generation path.
elements.sent-2411-088: Tree Search involves exploring multiple potential actions at each step of generation, allowing for a broader exploration of the state-action space.
elements.sent-2411-089: For instance, techniques like Beam Search and ToT \cite{yao2024tree incorporate tree structures to enhance exploration, improving model performance in reasoning tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-090: TouT \cite{mo2023treeuncertainthoughtsreasoning introduces uncertainty measurement based on Monte Carlo dropout, providing a more accurate evaluation of intermediate reasoning processes.}
elements.sent-2411-091: This approach significantly improves the likelihood of discovering global optimal solution, particularly in environments with complex state spaces.
elements.sent-2411-092: By simultaneously considering multiple paths, tree search mitigates the risk of being locked into sub-optimal decisions made early on, making it more robust in guiding the model toward an optimal outcome.
elements.sent-2411-093: However, this increased exploration comes at a higher computational cost, making tree search more suitable for scenarios when the optimal path is challenging to identify.
elements.sent-2411-094: To make tree search effective, the model must be continuously verified and prioritize paths that align better with the goal conditions.
elements.sent-2411-095: While search structures provide the foundational framework for navigating the state-action space, further enhancement is also crucial to enhance search performance.
elements.sent-2411-096: These enhancements address challenges such as balancing exploration and exploitation, escaping local optima, and improving the diversity of generated results.
elements.sent-2411-097: The enhancement strategies can be broadly categorized into two approaches: adjusting exploration parameters and intervening in the original state.
elements.sent-2411-098: Techniques such as Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS), Beam Search, and Reject Sampling focus on refining the exploration process by adjusting parameters like temperature, Top-k~\citep{fan2018hierarchicalneuralstorygeneration , or Top-p~\cite{holtzman2020curiouscaseneuraltext .}}
elements.sent-2411-099: The challenge lies in balancing the trade-off between generating diverse outputs and maintaining high-quality sequences.
elements.sent-2411-100: For example, increasing the temperature parameter promotes greater randomness, fostering diversity but potentially reducing coherence.
elements.sent-2411-101: Another enhancement approach involves modifying the initial state to guide the search process toward specific goals.
elements.sent-2411-102: Methods like Chain of Thought (CoT) \cite{wei2022chain , Logical Inference via Neurosymbolic Computation (LINC) \cite{Olausson_2023 , and Program of Thoughts (PoT) \cite{chen2023programthoughtspromptingdisentangling exemplify this strategy.}}}
elements.sent-2411-103: These interventions address the challenge of overcoming biases in the default state distribution.
elements.sent-2411-104: CoT enhances reasoning by introducing intermediate steps, improving interpretability and depth in generated sequences.
elements.sent-2411-105: LINC uses logical scenarios to encourage more structured and goal-oriented outputs.
elements.sent-2411-106: Similarly, PoT provides programmatic examples that guide models toward systematic problem-solving, effectively expanding the scope of exploration beyond the original state distribution.. \renewcommand{\arraystretch {1.5.}}
elements.figure-2411-004: \linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{lllll} Verifier Type Verification Form Verify Granularity Verifier Source \multicolumn{1}{p{10.085em}}{ Extra Training} Golden Annotation Binary/Text Thought Step/Full Trajectory Program Based No Rule-based Binary/Text Thought Step/Full Trajectory Program Based No Code Interpreter Binary/Score/Text Token/Thought Step/Full Trajectory Program Based No ORM Binary/Score/Rank/Text Full Trajectory Model Based Yes Language Model Binary/Score/Rank/Text Thought Step/Full Trajectory Model Based Yes Tool Binary/Score/Rank/Text Token/Thought Step/Full Trajectory Program Based No Search Engine Text Thought Step/Full Trajectory Program Based No PRM Score Token/Thought Step Model Based Yes Knowledge Graph Text Thought Step/Full Trajectory Program Based No \end{tabular} } \caption{A comprehensive taxonomy of verifiers across four dimensions: verification form, verify granularity, verifier source, and the need for extra training.} \label{tab:verifiers}
elements.sent-2411-107: After obtaining the verification result, we aim to enhance the capabilities of the foundation model, we define this process as the feedback stage.
elements.sent-2411-108: In this paper, feedback specifically refers to enhancing the foundation model’s capabilities based on the verification results.
elements.sent-2411-109: The feedback stage is critical, as the effectiveness of the feedback method directly determines whether the foundation model's capabilities can be appropriately enhanced in response to the verification results.
elements.sent-2411-110: In this section, we explore how verifier engineering utilizes search algorithms and verifiers to feedback verification results on foundation models.
elements.sent-2411-111: To maximize the objective function $J(\pi)$, the distribution of the policy $\pi$ can be optimized by adjusting $s_t$ or the parameters of $\pi$.
elements.sent-2411-112: This leads to two distinct feedback approaches: training-based feedback and inference-based feedback.
elements.sent-2411-113: Training-based feedback involves updating model parameters using data efficiently obtained through searching and verifying.
elements.sent-2411-114: In contrast, inference-based feedback modifies the output distribution by incorporating search and verification results as auxiliary information during inference, without altering the model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-115: We categorize the common training strategies for training-based feedback into three types, based on the nature and organization of the data utilized:.
elements.sent-2411-116: Imitation Learning typically uses high-quality data selected by verifiers, employing supervised fine-tuning objectives like cross-entropy or knowledge distillation training objectives like Kullback-Leibler divergence~\cite{hinton2015distilling to optimize the model.}
elements.sent-2411-117: Various approaches are employed to enhance specific capabilities of the foundation model through imitation learning.
elements.sent-2411-118: To enhance mathematical reasoning capabilities in LLMs, approaches like STaR~\cite{zelikman2022star and RFT~\cite{yuan2023scaling use rule-based verifiers to compare solution outcomes, while WizardMath~\cite{luo2023wizardmath , MARIO~\cite{liao2024mario , and MetaMath~\cite{yu2023metamath leverage verifiers to select responses from advanced LLMs or human inputs.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-119: Other methods such as MAmmoTH~\cite{yue2023mammoth and MathCoder~\cite{wang2023mathcoder utilize programmatic tools to verify comprehensive and detailed solutions.}}
elements.sent-2411-120: For coding capability improvement, various methods including Code Alpaca~\cite{codealpaca , WizardCoder~\cite{luo2023wizardcoder , WaveCoder~\cite{yu2023wavecoder , and OpenCodeInterpreter~\cite{zheng2024opencodeinterpreter construct code instruction-following datasets by distilling knowledge from advanced LLMs.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-121: To enhance instruction-following abilities, approaches like LLaMA-GPT4~\cite{peng2023instruction , Baize~\cite{xu2023baize , and Ultrachat~\cite{ding2023enhancing employ verifiers to distill responses from advanced LLMs for supervised fine-tuning.}}}
elements.sent-2411-122: Other methods such as Deita~\cite{liu2024what and MoDS~\cite{du2023mods implement a pipeline of verifiers to check complexity, quality, and diversity before selecting suitable data for SFT.}}
elements.sent-2411-123: Through this approach, models learn to align their outputs with verifier-provided preferences.
elements.sent-2411-124: Various techniques are adopted to boost the foundation model's capabilities in specific areas through preference learning.
elements.sent-2411-125: For mathematical reasoning enhancement, MCTS-DPO~\cite{xie2024monte combines Monte Carlo Tree Search~\cite{coulom2006efficient,kocsis2006bandit with preference learning to generate and learn from step-level pairwise comparisons in an iterative online manner.}}
elements.sent-2411-126: For coding capability improvement, CodeUltraFeedback~\cite{weyssow2024codeultrafeedback constructs pairwise training data by using LLM verifiers to rank code outputs, then applies preference learning algorithms to optimize the model's performance.}
elements.sent-2411-127: For instruction-following enhancement, Self-Rewarding~\cite{yuan2024self enables models to generate their own verification results for creating pairwise comparison data, followed by iterative self-improvement using the DPO method.}
elements.sent-2411-128: Reinforcement Learning optimizes models using reward signals from verifiers.
elements.sent-2411-129: Through environmental interaction and policy updates using algorithms like PPO~\cite{schulman2017proximal , PPO-max~\cite{zheng2023secrets , models iteratively improve their generation quality.}}
elements.sent-2411-130: Multiple approaches are used to enhance the foundation model’s capabilities in specific domains using reinforcement learning.
elements.sent-2411-131: For mathematical reasoning enhancement, Math-Shepherd~\cite{wang2023math implements step-wise reward mechanisms to guide progressive improvement in mathematical problem-solving capabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-132: For coding capability improvement, methods like RLTF~\cite{liu2023rltf and PPOCoder~\cite{shojaee2023execution leverage code execution results as reward signals to guide models toward more effective coding solutions.}}
elements.sent-2411-133: For instruction-following enhancement, approaches like InstructGPT~\cite{ouyang2022training and Llama~\cite{touvron2023llama,touvron2023llama2,dubey2024llama employ reward models trained to evaluate response helpfulness, optimizing models for better instruction adherence.}}
elements.sent-2411-134: In inference-based feedback, we modify inputs or inference strategies to obtain better outputs without changing model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-135: This approach is divided into two categories based on the visibility of verification results to the model: verifier-guided feedback and verifier-aware feedback.
elements.sent-2411-136: In verifier-guided feedback, verifiers evaluate and select the most appropriate outputs from model-generated content without direct model interaction.
elements.sent-2411-137: For example, \citet{lightman2023let and \citet{snell2024scaling implement tree search algorithms guided by progress rewards, while ToT~\cite{yao2024tree employs language model verifiers to direct its tree search process.}}}
elements.sent-2411-138: In the realm of contrastive decoding~\cite{li2022contrastive,o2023contrastive , advanced language models serve as token logits verifiers to optimize output distributions.}
elements.sent-2411-139: Verifier-Aware feedback integrates verifier feedback directly into a model's operational context to enhance the content generation process.
elements.sent-2411-140: This approach allows the model to actively consider and incorporate verifier feedback while producing its output.
elements.sent-2411-141: Various strategies are employed to enhance specific capabilities of foundation models through verifier-aware feedback.
elements.sent-2411-142: For mathematical and coding enhancement, CRITIC~\cite{gou2023critic utilizes feedback from calculators and code interpreters to refine solutions, while Self-debug~\cite{chen2023teaching improves code quality through execution result analysis.}}
elements.sent-2411-143: For hallucination mitigation, approaches like ReAct~\cite{yao2022react , KGR~\cite{guan2024mitigating , and CRITIC~\cite{gou2023critic integrate continuous feedback from search engines and knowledge graphs to ensure factual accuracy.}}}
elements.sent-2411-144: In a similar vein, Self-Refine~\cite{madaan2024self employs language model verifiers to iteratively improve response quality.}
elements.sent-2411-145: In this section, we provide a detailed examination of the insights derived from our framework.
elements.sent-2411-146: We begin by revisiting SFT, DPO, and RLHF through the lens of verifier engineering.
elements.sent-2411-147: Subsequently, we conduct an independent analysis of each stage within the framework.
elements.sent-2411-148: Finally, we offer a systematic evaluation of potential challenges inherent to the framework as a whole.
elements.subsection-2411-007: Revisiting SFT, DPO and RLHF from Verifier Engineering
elements.sent-2411-149: Our proposed approach to verifier engineering provides a unified perspective on commonly used post-training algorithms, offering valuable insights into their mechanisms.
elements.figure-2411-005: \begin{minipage}{0.13\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/sft.pdf} (a) SFT \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.13\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/dpo.pdf} (b) DPO \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.13\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/rlhf.pdf} (c) RLHF \end{minipage} \caption{A verifier engineering perspective on SFT, DPO, and RLHF: gray nodes represent sample paths not used in training, while non-gray nodes represent sample paths actively used in the training process.} \label{fig:perspective}
elements.sent-2411-150: SFT generates candidate responses by employing a linear search strategy that adheres to a singular search path defined by its training data.
elements.sent-2411-151: Throughout this process, the verifier classifies each token along the search path as a positive signal, while treating all other tokens as negatives.
elements.sent-2411-152: Subsequently, the foundational model is optimized through imitation learning using a cross-entropy loss function.
elements.sent-2411-153: Similarly, DPO employs a linear search strategy, maintaining only two distinct search paths: one corresponding to the ``chosen'' data derived from preference pairs and the other to the ``rejected'' data.
elements.sent-2411-154: The verifier treats the path associated with the chosen data as positive signals and the path associated with the rejected data as negative signals.
elements.sent-2411-155: Subsequently, the foundational model is optimized through the application of pairwise loss functions.
elements.sent-2411-156: RLHF employs a linear search strategy during the search stage, which is further enhanced by adjusting parameters such as Top-p and temperature to promote exploration within the candidate response generation process.
elements.sent-2411-157: As depicted in Figure~\ref{fig:perspective (c), after search, a value function is utilized to assign scores to each state within the generation trajectory.}
elements.sent-2411-158: This scoring mechanism estimates the expected return, thereby informing and guiding the optimization process through PPO algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-159: By mapping these methods to the stages of verifier engineering, we gain a clearer understanding of how each approach in the search, verify, and feedback stage enhances foundation model capabilities.

elements.subsection-2411-008: Discussion on three stages of Verifier Engineering
elements.sent-2411-160: The three stages of verifier engineering play a distinct and critical role in enhancing the capabilities of foundation models.
elements.sent-2411-161: This discussion delves into current challenges and proposes future research directions, focusing on search efficiency, verifier design, and the effectiveness of feedback methods.
elements.sent-2411-162: The efficiency and effectiveness of candidate response generation are crucial for model performance.
elements.sent-2411-163: The challenge lies in balancing exploration and exploitation, as well as aligning the search process with specific optimization goals.
elements.sent-2411-164: In this subsection, we first discuss the trade-off between exploration and exploitation, and then discuss how goal-aware search can further optimize the search process.
elements.sent-2411-165: The balance between exploration and exploitation is a core issue in the search stage of verifier engineering.
elements.sent-2411-166: Given an instruction, we need to search for candidate responses.
elements.sent-2411-167: For example, in DPO scenarios, we aim to obtain pair-wise responses with different verification results.
elements.sent-2411-168: Regardless of the objective, how to sample desired responses through the trade-off between exploration and exploitation remains a key challenge.
elements.sent-2411-169: Exploration refers to exploring new action spaces to obtain verified results with larger variance candidate responses, while exploitation utilizes known effective strategies to obtain responses that meet expected validation results.
elements.sent-2411-170: Exploration helps foundation models better understand the outcomes of different actions, while exploitation can obtain satisfactory responses at a lower cost.
elements.sent-2411-171: However, recent research~\cite{wilson2024llm,nguyen2024balancing,murthy2024rexrapidexplorationexploitation suggests that existing methods in the post-training process often rely too heavily on exploitation while neglecting the importance of exploration.}
elements.sent-2411-172: This imbalance may cause foundation models to get stuck in local optima, limiting their generalization ability.
elements.sent-2411-173: Therefore, how to better balance these two strategies during training is crucial for improving model performance.
elements.sent-2411-174: As shown in Section~\ref{sec:search , existing search methods primarily rely on probability search from the next token distribution and adjust the search direction through verifier feedback.}
elements.sent-2411-175: However, this approach has a fundamental issue: the current search approach often lacks a direct correlation with the optimization goal.
elements.sent-2411-176: Consequently, we cannot guide foundation models to generate candidate responses that meet our goals during the search stage and can only rely on post-hoc verification results to filter responses that align with our intentions.
elements.sent-2411-177: This delayed verification mechanism significantly reduces search efficiency and increases computational overhead.
elements.sent-2411-178: To address this challenge, search algorithms like A*~\citep{wang2009finding fuse the cost of the current node with heuristic estimates of the future cost to the goal.}
elements.sent-2411-179: Future work can focus on developing more robust goal estimation and fusion methods.
elements.sent-2411-180: Designing effective verifiers is pivotal for enhancing the performance and reliability of foundation models.
elements.sent-2411-181: However, several unresolved issues remain regarding the optimal design and integration of verifiers.
elements.sent-2411-182: In this subsection, we first examine the considerations involved in verifier design and the need for systematic evaluation frameworks.
elements.sent-2411-183: Subsequently, we explore the complexities associated with combining multiple verifiers, highlighting the challenges that must be addressed to achieve comprehensive and scalable verification systems.
elements.sent-2411-184: Designing specific verifiers for different types of instructions is essential.
elements.sent-2411-185: For instructions with explicit output constraints (such as grammatical format and length limitations), rule-based verifiers should be implemented due to their definitive reliability.
elements.sent-2411-186: For question-answering instructions, LLM-based verifiers are typically more effective in evaluating subjective metrics like response fluency and information content.
elements.sent-2411-187: However, recent studies~\cite{wen2024rethinking have revealed only weak correlations between existing verifier evaluation metrics and downstream task performance, highlighting significant limitations in the current verifier evaluation framework.}
elements.sent-2411-188: Therefore, a systematic evaluation framework is needed to comprehensively assess the effectiveness, applicable scope, and limitations of different types of verifiers.
elements.sent-2411-189: Such a framework would not only guide the selection and combination of verifiers but also establish best practices for verifier deployment across various task scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-190: As mentioned above, it is impossible to obtain effective verification results using a single verifier alone.
elements.sent-2411-191: Therefore, integrating multiple verifiers is essential to address the diverse requirements of candidate response evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-192: To comprehensively enhance foundation model performance across various task scenarios, an effective verifier combination system must be developed.
elements.sent-2411-193: Building an effective verifier combination system faces three key challenges:.
elements.sent-2411-194: Instruction Coverage: The verifier combination must be capable of handling various types of instructions to ensure the completeness of the evaluation system.
elements.sent-2411-195: Building a comprehensive verifier framework requires a deep understanding of different task characteristics and evaluation needs, including structured output validation, open-ended question assessment, creative task evaluation, etc..
elements.sent-2411-196: Automatic Routing Mechanism: Different tasks typically require verifiers with varying forms and granularity, demanding an intelligent verifier routing system.
elements.sent-2411-197: This system needs to analyze instruction characteristics and select appropriate verifier combinations accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-198: Based on the PAC theory discussed in Section 2, the chosen verifier combinations should effectively approximate our ultimate optimization objective.
elements.sent-2411-199: Verification Results Integration Strategy: When multiple verifiers produce different verification results, a reliable decision-making mechanism is needed.
elements.sent-2411-200: This includes dynamic adjustment of different verifier weights, conflict resolution strategies, and methods for synthesizing final scores.
elements.sent-2411-201: Especially, when there are differences in verification results, factors such as the credibility and task relevance of each verifier need to be considered to make reasonable judgments.
elements.sent-2411-202: The effectiveness of feedback methods plays a crucial role in shaping the performance and adaptability of foundation models.
elements.sent-2411-203: In particular, we focus on two key questions: 1) whether the feedback method can accurately and efficiently improve the model's performance, and 2) whether it can generalize effectively to other queries.
elements.paragraph-2411-011: Key Factors in Designing Feedback Methods
elements.sent-2411-204: Different feedback methods have different impacts on foundation model capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-205: When selecting feedback methods, it is essential to carefully balance several key factors.
elements.sent-2411-206: Firstly, the algorithm should demonstrate sufficient robustness to noise in verification results, ensuring the stability of the feedback process.
elements.sent-2411-207: Secondly, it is crucial to assess how the feedback algorithm based on specific types of verification results impacts the model.
elements.sent-2411-208: Over-optimizing certain capabilities can undermine the model's fundamental capabilities and overall generalization, potentially leading to performance degradation.
elements.sent-2411-209: Furthermore, foundation models with different capacities may require distinct optimization approaches.
elements.sent-2411-210: Larger models might benefit from more sophisticated feedback methods, while smaller models may need more conservative feedback methods to prevent capacity saturation.
elements.sent-2411-211: It is crucial to find an appropriate balance between these factors while considering the model's inherent capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-212: Equipped with reliable verifiers and effective feedback methods, the ideal scenario is to achieve comprehensive improvements in foundation model capabilities through optimization on a limited set of queries.
elements.sent-2411-213: This necessitates feedback methods to possess strong cross-query generalization abilities.
elements.sent-2411-214: Specifically, when we enhance certain foundation model capabilities through feedback on specific queries, these improved capabilities should effectively transfer and persist when handling novel queries.
elements.sent-2411-215: However, generalization also faces significant challenges: different queries may require the model to invoke distinct capabilities, and incorrect generalization might lead to the model inappropriately applying certain capabilities in unsuitable scenarios, potentially degrading performance.
elements.sent-2411-216: Therefore, feedback methods must not only promote effective generalization but also prevent over-generalization and incorrect transfer of capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-217: In this section, we will discuss the problems we may meet in verifier engineering implementation in different stages to ensure efficiency, scalability, and reliability.
elements.sent-2411-218: Achieving high search efficiency is a key objective in verifier engineering.
elements.sent-2411-219: Excessive search often slows down the entire verifier engineering pipeline.
elements.sent-2411-220: To address this, most current LLM-based PPO algorithms sample only a single response for optimization.
elements.sent-2411-221: On the other hand, RLHF-like algorithms commonly incorporate importance sampling~\cite{schulman2017proximal,xie2019towards techniques to enhance search efficiency, minimizing the need for frequent switching between search, verify, and feedback stages while simultaneously improving sample utilization.}
elements.sent-2411-222: Verifier efficiency is also a key goal in verifier engineering for giving timely and effective verification results.
elements.sent-2411-223: When handling multiple instructions from various sources, it's crucial to employ different combinations of verifiers with different abilities to ensure accurate verification results.
elements.sent-2411-224: Determining the optimal approach to deploy all verifiers online and dynamically schedule them each time to minimize resource consumption while maximizing efficiency presents a challenging problem.
elements.sent-2411-225: Delivering effective verification results involves addressing two major challenges: (1) ensuring the verifier’s knowledge remains synchronized with the policy model, and (2) selecting the optimal verifier combination when capabilities vary or conflict across verifiers.
elements.sent-2411-226: For instance, InstructGPT~\cite{ouyang2022training employs a human-annotated reward model as a verifier, and to counter the limitations of a static verifier, it periodically re-annotates reward model data to align its evaluation capabilities with the evolving policy model outputs.}
elements.sent-2411-227: Furthermore, \citet{quan2024dmoerm leverages a Mixture of Experts architecture to combine multiple verifiers with different strengths.}
elements.sent-2411-228: Experiential Co-Learning~\cite{qian2023experiential also draws on the knowledge of diverse foundation models to provide more robust verification results.}
elements.sent-2411-229: For highly efficient feedback, it’s essential not only to enhance the feedback algorithm itself but also to optimize the entire workflow.
elements.sent-2411-230: To increase training and inference efficiency, LoRA~\cite{hu2021lora,xin-etal-2024-beyond improves training efficiency by reducing the number of trainable parameters and vLLM~\cite{kwon2023efficient enhance inference efficiency.}}
elements.sent-2411-231: To optimize the entire workflow, deciding when to apply feedback methods is crucial~\cite{tang2024understanding .}
elements.sent-2411-232: For training-based feedback, understanding the performance gap between online and offline feedback methods is key.
elements.sent-2411-233: Research has shown that online feedback methods can maximize the capabilities of foundation models by providing timely verification results on the on-policy candidate responses, albeit requiring frequent sampling, which can be time-intensive.
elements.sent-2411-234: In contrast, offline feedback methods enable comprehensive response exploration by leveraging pre-prepared datasets for training, thereby streamlining the process.
elements.sent-2411-235: However, this approach tends to have lower data utilization efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-236: This highlights the importance of balancing online and offline feedback methods.
elements.sent-2411-237: In inference-based feedback, deciding when to call a verifier is essential.
elements.sent-2411-238: This paper introduces the concept of verifier engineering and explores the significant shift in research paradigms from feature engineering to data engineering, and ultimately to verifier engineering.
elements.sent-2411-239: Our framework provides implications and insights demonstrating that verifier engineering can optimize the capabilities of foundation models through a closed-loop feedback cycle encompassing search, verification, and feedback.
elements.sent-2411-240: Furthermore, we categorize existing search algorithms based on their granularity and schemes, review current verifiers, and classify feedback methods from both training-based and inference-based perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-241: Finally, we discuss the challenges that verifier engineering currently faces.
elements.sent-2411-242: Through this paper, we aim to stimulate further discussions and promote practical applications in the field of verifier engineering for achieving Artificial General Intelligence.. \bibliographystyle{icml2024.}
